agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an e-commerce/order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled. The requirements create a logical impossibility: orders are invoiced at placement, invoiced orders cannot be cancelled, yet the PM expects cancellation to be available until shipment.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A customer's request to purchase goods, tracked through a lifecycle of states from placement to shipment and beyond."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order so it will not be fulfilled. Intended to be available before shipment per PM requirements."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document that locks in pricing and records the obligation to pay. Described by Finance as irreversible once created."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer. The PM's stated boundary after which cancellation is no longer permitted."},{"term":"Order Placement","definition":"The moment a customer confirms an order. Operations states invoicing occurs at this point."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order may be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy from the PM defining the cancellation window. A VP could change this policy (e.g., restrict cancellation to within 1 hour of placement).","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, when a cancellation is requested, then the cancellation succeeds."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"An invoiced order cannot be cancelled; invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats invoicing as an irreversible financial commitment. Reversing an invoice has accounting, tax, and audit implications — this is a data integrity constraint, not a policy preference.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has been invoiced, when a cancellation is requested, then the cancellation is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy decision about when to invoice. The business could choose to invoice at a different point (e.g., at shipment) without corrupting system state.","testable_assertion":"Given a new order is placed, when placement is confirmed, then an invoice is immediately generated for that order."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"An order has been placed and therefore invoiced (per BR-3)","when":"A cancellation request is submitted before shipment","then":"CONTRADICTION: BR-1 says cancel succeeds, BR-2 says cancel is rejected. Cannot define expected behavior until stakeholders resolve the conflict."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"An order has shipped","when":"A cancellation request is submitted","then":"The cancellation is rejected (all three stakeholders agree on this)"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An order is placed","when":"Placement is confirmed","then":"An invoice is generated and the order is marked as invoiced"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed but invoice generation fails (system error)","expected_behavior":"Ambiguous — if the order exists without an invoice, BR-1 would allow cancellation but BR-3 implies this state should not exist. Needs error-handling policy."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not","expected_behavior":"Unclear whether cancellation applies to the unshipped portion. PM requirement says 'before it ships' without addressing partial shipment."},{"scenario":"Credit note or refund issued against an invoice","expected_behavior":"Finance says invoicing is irreversible, but standard accounting practice allows credit notes. The distinction between cancelling an invoice vs. issuing a credit note needs clarification."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If the contradiction is resolved by allowing cancellation of invoiced orders, Finance must define a reversal mechanism (credit notes, voids) with tax and revenue recognition implications. If resolved by blocking cancellation, customer satisfaction and return rates will be affected.","operational":"Operations invoices at placement to lock pricing. If the resolution requires deferring invoicing, Operations loses price-lock capability and must handle price fluctuation risk. If cancellation is allowed with credit notes, Operations gains a more complex workflow.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment (PM requirement). If invoicing blocks cancellation, customers will experience unexpected friction, likely increasing support tickets and complaints.","risk":"Implementing any interpretation without resolving the contradiction risks either financial integrity violations (if invoiced orders are cancelled without proper accounting) or customer experience degradation (if cancellation is blocked). Regulatory risk exists if invoice handling violates local tax/accounting laws.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders at all under current rules, contradicting reasonable expectations set by the PM's stated policy."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"Must define whether invoice reversal (credit notes) is acceptable, and if so, under what conditions."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"May need to change invoicing timing if the resolution requires deferring invoicing to a later lifecycle stage."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of customer complaints if cancellation is blocked; needs clear policy to communicate to customers."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"Must reconcile the cancellation policy with financial and operational constraints."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"Three explicit, named stakeholder positions create a logical impossibility. Combining BR-2 and BR-3 makes BR-1 unsatisfiable: every order is invoiced at placement, and no invoiced order can be cancelled, so no order can ever be cancelled — directly contradicting the PM's requirement. The spec quality is low because no stakeholder has acknowledged or addressed this conflict. Resolution requires a deliberate business decision, not a technical interpretation.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and not a matter of interpretation — it is a logical consequence of the three stated rules","Post-shipment cancellation is universally blocked (all stakeholders agree)","Invoicing at placement is current operational practice"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether Finance would accept credit notes as a workaround distinct from 'cancelling an invoice'","Whether Operations is flexible on invoicing timing","Whether the PM was aware of the invoicing constraint when stating the cancellation policy"],"assumptions":["All three stakeholder statements are current and authoritative","'Invoice' means the same thing to Finance and Operations (same bounded context)","There is no existing credit-note or invoice-void mechanism that stakeholders omitted from their requirements"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-1 (cancel before shipment) is logically unsatisfiable when combined with BR-2 (invoiced orders cannot be cancelled) and BR-3 (orders are invoiced at placement). The transitive conclusion is that no order can ever be cancelled, which directly contradicts the PM's requirement. This is not an ambiguity — all three rules are explicitly stated, and they cannot all be true simultaneously.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers"],"options":["A: Defer invoicing to shipment time — preserves PM's cancellation window but Operations loses price-lock at placement","B: Allow cancellation of invoiced orders via credit notes — Finance must accept that 'cancellation' triggers a credit note rather than voiding the invoice, preserving the irreversibility of invoices while enabling the business workflow","C: Restrict cancellation to a short window before invoicing (e.g., minutes after placement) — a compromise but may be too narrow to be useful","D: Maintain Finance's constraint and remove the cancellation feature — simplest but worst customer experience"],"recommendation":"Option B is the most likely intended design. Finance's concern is accounting integrity (irreversible invoices), not blocking cancellations. A credit-note mechanism satisfies Finance's invariant while enabling the PM's cancellation policy. However, this requires explicit confirmation from Finance that credit notes are acceptable."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"The three requirements as stated are mutually exclusive and cannot be implemented as a coherent system.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1, BR-2, and BR-3 form a logical contradiction: BR-3 ensures all orders are invoiced immediately, BR-2 prevents cancellation of invoiced orders, and BR-1 requires cancellation to be possible before shipment."}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an e-commerce/order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled. The requirements create a logical impossibility: orders are invoiced at placement, invoiced orders cannot be cancelled, yet the PM expects cancellation to be available until shipment.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term

content.thinking:
Let me analyze these requirements carefully.

Three statements:
1. PM: "An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships."
2. Finance: "Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled — invoicing is irreversible."
3. Operations: "We invoice orders at the time of placement to lock in pricing."

The contradiction: If orders are invoiced at placement (ops), and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (finance), then orders can NEVER be cancelled — because they're invoiced immediately. This directly contradicts the PM's requirement that orders can be cancelled before shipping.

This is a clear contradiction between explicit stakeholder positions.

This is not an ambiguity — all three rules are explicitly stated, and they cannot all be true simultaneously.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers"],"options":["A: Defer invoicing to shipment time — preserves PM's cancellation window but Operations loses price-lock at placement","B: Allow cancellation of invoiced orders via credit notes — Finance must accept that 'cancellation' triggers a credit note rather than voiding the invoice, preserving the irreversibility of invoices while enabling the business workflow","C: Restrict cancellation to a short window before invoicing (e.g., minutes after placement) — a compromise but may be too narrow to be useful","D: Maintain Finance's constraint and remove the cancellation feature — simplest but worst customer experience"],"recommendation":"Option B is the most likely intended design.
